summary:
The [[event:1674|Liangzhu culture]] of the Yangtze River Delta is renowned for its exceptional jade craftsmanship, produced between approximately 3300 and 2300 BCE. Liangzhu artisans shaped nephrite jade into distinctive ritual objects—most notably the cong (a cylindrical tube with a square exterior), the bi (a flat disc with a central hole), and the yue (a battle axe)—using stone and abrasive sand tools in the absence of metal. These forms became canonical in Chinese ritual culture for thousands of years afterward.

Jade artifacts were deposited in elite burials in great quantities, indicating that control over jade was closely tied to social and religious authority. The cong in particular is decorated with a recurrent 'deity-animal face' motif whose meaning remains debated but may represent a shamanic or cosmological symbol. The quality and quantity of Liangzhu jade work is unmatched among contemporary world cultures, and the tradition exerted a profound long-term influence on Chinese ritual aesthetics and statecraft.

read_more_links.title: Jade in Chinese Culture - Metropolitan Museum
